coredata.dc:title: Mesoporous silica-encapsulated nitrogen-doped fluorescent carbon dots modified polydopamine composites for monitoring non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Chandel, Palvi
Sharma, Sunakshi
Mohiuddin, Irshad
Bhogal, Shikha
coredata.dc:description: A one-pot hydrothermal process incorporated nitrogen-doped fluorescent carbon dots (N-FCDs) into the pores of mesoporous silica (m-SiO2). Under weakly alkaline conditions, the m-SiO2/N-FCDs were further functionalized with dopamine to develop m-SiO2/N-FCDs modified polydopamine (m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA). The m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA exhibited emission at a wavelength of 520 nm (λexc. = 440 nm) and demonstrated excellent quenching efficiency in the presence of NSAIDs (aspirin, diclofenac, ketoprofen, naproxen, and ibuprofen) with a detection limit of 0.045 – 1.43 nM. Furthermore, the m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA was utilized to detect NSAIDs in tap and river water and pharmaceutical commercial tablets showing high recoveries of 90.96–100.41 % with RSD < 4.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mesoporous silica
Nitrogen-doped fluorescent carbon dots
Polydopamine
Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs
Fluorescent sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of mesoporous silica encapsulated nitrogen doped fluorescent carbon dots modified polydopamine
Synthesis of mesoporous silica encapsulated nitrogen doped fluorescent carbon dots modified polydopamine

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: University Centre for Research and Development of Chandigarh University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors (PC, SS, and SB) acknowledge the Department of Chemistry, Chandigarh University, Gharaun, Mohali, for providing lab and instrumental facilities. SB would also like to thank the University Centre for Research and Development of Chandigarh University, Gharaun, Mohali, India, for instrumental facilities. All authors have read and approved the manuscript. Not applicable. Not applicable

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the different steps involved in the synthesis of mesoporous silica encapsulated nitrogen-doped fluorescent carbon dots modified polydopamine nanocomposite.
FTIR spectra of the synthesized materials: a) m-SiO2, b) m-SiO2/N-FCDs, and c) m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA.

HRTEM images depicting the morphology of the synthesized materials: a-c) m-SiO2, d-f) N-FCDs, g-i), m-SiO2/N-FCDs, and j-l) m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA; SAED images of m) m-SiO2, n) m-SiO2/N-FCDs, and o) m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA illustrating the amorphous nature.

N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm properties of the m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA: a) isotherm curve, b) BJH pore size curve, and muti-point BET graph; TGA and DTG curves of the synthesized materials: d) m-SiO2, e) m-SiO2/N-FCDs, and f) m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA.
XPS spectra of the m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA: a) XPS survey spectrum and high-definition spectra of b) C 1 s, c) N 1 s, d) O 1 s, and e) Si 2p.

Fluorescence emission spectra of the m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA in the presence of different concentrations of NSAIDs: a) ASP (0–330 nM), b) DCF (0–200 nM), c) KPF (0–235 nM), d) NPX (0–260 nM), and e) IBU (0–290 nM).
Binding mechanism illustrates the possible interactions exist between NSAIDs (model analyte: ASP) and m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA.
Non-linear Stern-Volmer plots for different NSAIDs: a) ASP, b) DCF, c) KPF, d) NPX, and e) IBU.
Determination of NSAIDs in different real samples (tap water, river water, and pharmaceutical tablets by the m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA.
Performance comparison of the developed method (mSiO2/N-FCDs/PDA) with the other reported methods in the literature.

row.entry: SPE/Acidic calix[4]arene intercalated in Mg/Al LDH
Acetaminophen, caffeine, naproxen, celecoxib, ibuprofen and mefenamic acid
Ketoprofen, naproxen, fenbufen, diclofenac, and ibuprofen
Tap water, river water, and urine
Pharmaceutical tablets, tap water, and river water
Aspirin, diclofenac, ketoprofen, naproxen, and ibuprofen

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: SPE – solid phase extraction; DSPE – dispersive – solid phase extraction; MIP@CDs-molecularly imprinted polymer carbon dots; ms-MIP-FCDs – mesoporous molecularly imprinted polymer fluorescent carbon dots.

head.ce:title: Mesoporous silica-encapsulated nitrogen-doped fluorescent carbon dots modified polydopamine composites for monitoring non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Chandigarh University, Gharuan, Mohali, Punjab 140413, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Chandigarh University, Gharuan, Mohali, Punjab 140413, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Panjab University, Sector-14, Chandigarh 160014, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Panjab University, Sector-14, Chandigarh 160014, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University Centre for Research and Development, Chandigarh University, Gharuan, Mohali, Punjab 140413, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University Centre for Research and Development

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: University Centre for Research and Development, Chandigarh University, Gharuan, Mohali, Punjab 140413, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A one-pot hydrothermal process incorporated nitrogen-doped fluorescent carbon dots (N-FCDs) into the pores of mesoporous silica (m-SiO2). Under weakly alkaline conditions, the m-SiO2/N-FCDs were further functionalized with dopamine to develop m-SiO2/N-FCDs modified polydopamine (m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA). The m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA exhibited emission at a wavelength of 520 nm (λexc. = 440 nm) and demonstrated excellent quenching efficiency in the presence of NSAIDs (aspirin, diclofenac, ketoprofen, naproxen, and ibuprofen) with a detection limit of 0.045 – 1.43 nM. Furthermore, the m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA was utilized to detect NSAIDs in tap and river water and pharmaceutical commercial tablets showing high recoveries of 90.96–100.41 % with RSD < 4.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The presence of pharmaceutical residues in the environment has long-term and immediate impacts on ecological systems, even in low concentrations [1]. Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) are among the most often used medications and have emerged as major contributors to environmental pollution [2,3]. NSAIDs function as anti-inflammatory, analgesic, and antipyretics to treat illness in both humans and animals. The NSAIDs were chosen as the analyte of interest for three primary reasons: i) NSAIDs are the most often prescribed medicinal drugs both with prescription and for self-medication [4]. NSAIDs are the most frequently detected drug in the aquatic environment, with the highest concentration level being reported in Europe [5]; ii) NSAIDs can persist in the aqueous phase because of their distinctive properties of hydrophilicity and stability. For instance, naproxen (NPX) may remain in the environment, accumulate into organisms, and maintain its biological activity for a year [6]; and iii) the presence of NSAIDs in environmental water, even at very low concentrations (e.g., ng L−1) is thought to pose a concern to human health and aquatic ecosystems [7,8].

Currently, pharmaceutical residues, including NSAIDs, are typically identified through off-site analysis, which involves the collection, transportation, and pre-treatment of samples, as well as the use of expensive instrumentation like high-performance liquid chromatography [9,10], gas chromatography [11,12], and specialized detection units, all operated by trained personnel. Therefore, introducing materials or devices capable of swiftly detecting target analytes would significantly enhance environmental monitoring and yield substantial economic advantages. The advancement of chemical sensors aims to overcome limitations associated with traditional analytical procedures and the reliance on conventional laboratory methods for environmental surveillance. Furthermore, using environmentally friendly precursors in constructing these sensors, particularly nanoprobe technology, is currently an area of active exploration.

Fluorescent carbon dots (FCDs) have emerged as a promising class of sensing nanoprobes because of their outstanding photoluminescent characteristics, straightforward synthesis, excellent water solubility, high thermal stability, low cytotoxicity, and exceptional photo-stability [13,14]. Compared to other materials, such as organic dyes and quantum dots, which pose concerns regarding photo-bleaching and toxicity, FCDs stand out as superior fluorophores [15]. Their remarkable attributes make them ideally suited for detecting a wide range of chemical substances, from simple organic molecules to complex structural frameworks [16].

FCDs have been applied as fluorescent probes for detecting many pharmaceutical drugs, including NSAIDs, tetracycline antibiotics, veterinary drugs, and anticancer drugs [17–21]. For example, red emissive carbon dots (R-CDs) was synthesized for the sensing of veterinary drugs (malachite green oxalate, 1-aminohydantoin hydrochloride, and pefloxacin) [22]. R-CDs were synthesized using a hydrothermal approach utilizing sorbitol and o-phenylenediamine as the precursors. The interaction of drugs containing different functional groups (–COOH or –NH3 +) and R-CDs causes fluorescence quenching. In another study, R-CDs have developed for the turn on determination of tetracycline antibiotics (tetracycline, oxytetracycline, and chlortetracycline) [23]. The R-CDs displayed a remarkable emission enhancement upon introducing tetracyclines based on an aggregation-induced emission enhancement mechanism with a limit of detections of 12–25 nM. A highly selective material based on carbon dots and molecularly imprinted polymers was utilized to detect ketoprofen [21]. The synthesized fluorescence probe was selectively quenched in the presence of ketoprofen compared to the other related compounds (naproxen, ibuprofen, and fenoprofen).

FCDs exhibit limitations such as size and structure variability [24,25]. Researchers have explored doping with phosphorus, nitrogen, sulfur, etc., or encapsulating FCDs within mesoporous silica (m-SiO2) to address these challenges, offering improved stability, controlled size, and enhanced surface functionalization [26,27]. However, further optimization is sought to mitigate aggregation and improve biocompatibility. Introducing polydopamine (PDA) coatings onto mesoporous silica-encapsulated FCDs presents a promising approach to tackling these issues comprehensively. PDA is a polymer of the natural biomolecule dopamine (DA), generally found in mussel adhesive proteins [28]. The self-polymerization ability of DA under basic conditions generates a network with plenty of functional groups such as imines, o-hydroxyl, and amines, which endues the possibility of interacting with the analytes [29,30]. PDA enhances stability and dispersibility and provides the stronger binding of target analytes through multiple interactions.

Through this sequential modification strategy, we aimed to create a versatile composite material with tailored properties suitable for sensing five commonly used NSAIDs (namely, aspirin (ASP), diclofenac (DCF), ketoprofen (KPF), NPX, and ibuprofen (IBF) in various environmental components. As far as current research is concerned, this is the first detailed attempt to monitor NSAIDs via fluorescence sensing using mesoporous silica encapsulated nitrogen doped fluorescent carbon dots modified polydopamine composites (m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA). This sensing probe synthesis procedure benefits from many green chemistry attributes such as safe reagents, reduced waste, low cost, and reduced use of glassware. The proposed method can help to improve the performance of sensing technology for the accurate analysis of various target analytes, especially NSAIDs, including ASP, DCF, KPF, NPX, and IBF present in aqueous matrices. Our intensive efforts and substantial experimental results are expected to shed new light on the interaction of NSAIDs with m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA composites and their advanced applications for environmental monitoring.
Sections S1 and S2 illustrate all the information related to chemicals and instrumentation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of mesoporous silica encapsulated nitrogen doped fluorescent carbon dots modified polydopamine
ce:section.ce:para: The synthetic procedure of mesoporous silica encapsulated nitrogen doped fluorescent carbon dots modified polydopamine involves three significant steps: i) synthesis of m-SiO2; ii) encapsulation of N-doped FCDs into the pores of m-SiO2 (m-SiO2/N-FCDs); and iii) surface modification of m SiO2/N-FCDs with polydopamine (m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA).

For m-SiO2, cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) (0.160 g) was dissolved in distilled water (DW; 100 mL). Next, NaOH (0.5 mL; 2 mol/L) was added, and the mixture was stirred for 15 min at 80 °C. Afterward, tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS; 1 mL) was added to the above solution, and stirring was continued for 2 h. The obtained particles were centrifuged and washed with DW before being dried in an oven. Finally, m-SiO2 was obtained by refluxing the particles in HCl/methanol for 3 h to remove the surfactant, i.e., CTAB.

N-FCDs/m-SiO2 were prepared by dispersing the m-SiO2 in an uniformly aqueous solution (80 mL) by ultrasonication. Thereafter, citric acid (3 g) and urea (1 mg) were added and thoroughly mixed with continuous stirring. The mixed solution underwent a hydrothermal reaction for five hours at 220 °C, generating N-FCDs in situ into the pores of m-SiO2. The precipitate was centrifuged, washed, and dried at 60 °C in an oven to create the m-SiO2/N-FCDs nanocomposites. For comparison, the N-FCDs were prepared using the same method but without adding m-SiO2. For the surface functionalization of m-SiO2/N-FCDs with PDA, 100 mg of m-SiO2/N-FCDs were dispersed in Tris-buffer solution (pH 8.5; 20 mL) and ethanol (20 mL). The mixture was stirred for 30 min, and dopamine (10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 mg) was added while continuously stirring for 24 h at room temperature. Lastly, the PDA-coated m-SiO2/N-FCDs dark slurry-like product (denoted as m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA) was separated by centrifugation (10,000 rpm). After that, it was rinsed with DW to remove any remaining unpolymerized dopamine, and it was dried in an oven at 80 °C for six hours.

Establishing a quality assurance protocol is imperative to ensure the analytical findings are reliable and valuable. Before being used, all laboratory glassware was carefully cleaned with DW, acetone, and methanol. After that, it was covered in clean aluminum foil and dried at 70 °C for three hours. Three analyses were conducted on real samples, spiked samples, and procedure blanks. A standard solution of NSAIDs (3 mg) was prepared by dissolving in 3 mL of methanol. Stock solutions were stored at 4 °C- in screw-capped vials. The working standard solutions of the desired concentration were produced by gradually diluting the standard stock solutions in DW to obtain the final concentration.

To a 5 mL glass vial, 500 µL of m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA, 100 μL of phosphate buffer solution (pH = 6.0), and 1 mL of NSAID solution were added. The final volume of the solution was adjusted to 5 mL with the addition of DW. The fluorescence intensity of the solution was checked at 520 nm (λexc. = 440 nm) after incubation for 12 min.

In order to optimize the amount of m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA, various quantities ranging from 100 to 600 µL (1 mg of m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA in 10 mL DMSO) were taken in a 5 mL solution containing 100 μL of phosphate buffer solution (pH = 6.0) and 1 mL of ASP, DCF, KPF, NPX, and IBU (33–55 nM). The response time was optimized by taking a 5 mL solution of m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA containing 500 µL of m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA, 100 μL of phosphate buffer solution (pH = 6.0) and 1 mL of ASP, DCF, KPF, NPX, and IBU (33 – 55 nM) at different time intervals. Similarly, a 5 mL solution containing 500 µL of m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA and ASP, DCF, KPF, NPX, and IBU (33 – 55 nM) were prepared at different pH values. The spectrofluorimetric response of the solution was recorded at 520 nm (λexc. = 440 nm).

Different real samples were taken to analyze target NSAIDs, including river and tap water and commercial pharmaceutical tablets. The tap water was collected from our lab at Chandigarh University, Gharoun, Mohali, Punjab, India. River water samples were taken from Satluj River, Morinda, Punjab, during the spring of 2024. Water samples were collected in amber Pyrex borosilicate glass vials, which were then thoroughly cleaned with DW after being pre-washed with diluted HNO3.

Commercial pharmaceutical tablets of ASP, DCF, KPF, NPX, and IBU were purchased from a local pharmacy in Chandigarh, India. Five tablets of each NSAID were ground to make powders. Afterward, 100 mg of each powdered sample of NSAIDs was dissolved in methanol. The obtained mixture was filtered to get a clear solution and transferred into a 100 mL volumetric flask. The pharmaceutical solutions' working standards were prepared using this clear supernatant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of mesoporous silica encapsulated nitrogen doped fluorescent carbon dots modified polydopamine
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the m-SiO2 was synthesized using CTAB and TEOS as the structure-directing agent and silica precursor, respectively. After the formation of m-SiO2, the surfactant was removed by the reflux in the HCl/MeOH solution. N-FCDs were then encapsulated into the pores of m-SiO2 using a one-pot hydrothermal technique to create m-SiO2/N-FCDs, utilizing urea and citric acid as the nitrogen and carbon precursors, respectively. The surface of m-SiO2/N-FCDs was then functionalized via self-polymerization using DA, resulting in PDA under weakly alkaline conditions. PDA-modified m-SiO2/N-FCDs (m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA) offer significantly more active anchoring sites, including amines and catechols, which could allow a stronger binding with the target NSAIDs (Fig. 1 ). Different amounts of DA (10 – 50 mg) were utilized in the preparation of m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA to evaluate the effect of DA on the detection of NSAIDs. The quenching efficiency of NSAIDs increased with the increase in DA, and maximum quenching efficiency was achieved at 40 mg of DA (Figure S1). An increase in DA would cause more PDA functional groups to have access to NSAIDs, which would increase NSAID quenching. On the other hand, a persistent increase in DA concentration results in steric hindrance between functional groups and inadequate NSAID binding, thereby decreasing quenching.

The structural information of m-SiO2, m-SiO2/N-FCDs, and m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA was furnished by FT-IR, HR-TEM, EDS, N2 physiosorption, TGA, and XPS. FT-IR was applied to determine the kind of functional groups that were present in the synthesized materials. For m-SiO2, the observed peak from Fig. 2 a around 1067 and 958 cm−1 suggests Si-O-Si and Si-O-H stretching vibrations, respectively. The 799 and 451 cm−1 bands result from Si–O vibrations (Fig. 2a). m-SiO2 appears to have been prepared successfully. Compared with the spectra of pure m-SiO2, m-SiO2/N-FCDs (Fig. 2b) show the characteristic band of O-H stretch and N–H bending at 3390 and 1639 cm−1, respectively. The 2920 and 2851 cm−1 peaks are related to the C–H bond stretching vibrations. The FT-IR spectrum of m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA, the broad band at 3300 cm−1, the bands at 1606 and 1369 cm−1 are attributed to O–H stretching, superposition of phenylic CC stretch, and N-H bending, and aromatic C-H bending, respectively. This confirms the existence of m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA (Fig. 2c).

HR-TEM images were recorded to examine the structures of m-SiO2, N-FCDs, m-SiO2/N-FCDs, and m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA. HR-TEM images verify the mesoporous structures of m-SiO2 (Fig. 3 a–c). N-FCDs are spherical with particle size of 2 – 5 nm (Fig. 3d–f). The pores of m-SiO2 tend to disappear slightly after N-FCDs loading (Fig. 3g–i). The rough shells observed in the images of m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA (Fig. 3j–l) provided additional proof of the successful fabrication of the PDA layer with m-SiO2/N-FCDs. The SAED patterns of m-SiO2, m-SiO2/N-FCDs, and m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA display diffused rings demonstrating the amorphous phase of these materials (Fig. 3m – o).

EDS analysis was employed to characterize the elemental composition of m-SiO2, m-SiO2/N-FCDs, and m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA (Fig. S2a-c). C, O, N, and Si were dispersed in the analyzed materials to keep with our overall assumptions. In the EDS layered image of m-SiO2, C, O, and Si are present, as shown in the map data in Fig. S1a. After loading the N-FCDs to the m-SiO2 and further fabrication of the PDA layer, the EDS layered image indicates that the N peak appeared, corresponding to the N element in m-SiO2/N-FCDs and m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA (Fig. S1b and c). Such findings show that PDA with the m-SiO2/N-FCDs is formed quite effectively.

The textural characteristics of m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA were investigated using the nitrogen gas physio-sorption isotherm. The specific area of the surface and distribution of pore sizes were calculated using the multi-point BET model and the BJH method, respectively. Fig. 4 a – c shows the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm curve, pore size distribution, and surface area of the m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA. The mesoporous structure of m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA is supported by the adsorption–desorption isotherms, which, by IUPAC standards, belong to the Type II branch (H3 hysteresis loop). The surface area of m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA was 517.232 m2/g, which showed that m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA had increased available surface area because of porous cavities. Furthermore, the pore volume, BJH pore diameter, and average pore diameter values are 1.056 cm3 g−1, 2.913 nm, and 8.33 nm for the m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA, respectively. The pore size distribution analysis further confirmed that the m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA contain mesopores (1–16 nm pore diameter) in their structures.

The thermal stability of the m-SiO2, m-SiO2/N-FCDs, and m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA was validated by TGA and DTG curves by varying the temperature from 35-800 °C. For m-SiO2, the first weight loss was obtained at 5 % below 150 °C, possibly due to the loss of physio-adsorbed water molecules. The next weight loss between 200–300 °C was ascribed to the breakdown of organic groups on the surface of m-SiO2 (Fig. 4d). For m-SiO2/N-FCDs, two weight losses appeared; the first one was due to the evaporation of water molecules (below 100 °C), and the second loss of 5 % between 150–500 °C was mainly ascribed to the decomposition of N-FCDs (Fig. 4e). For the m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA, the initial decline of weight (14 %) below 250 °C was due to the water molecules and organic functional groups. The subsequent weight loss of 25 % below 500 °C might be due to the thermal decomposition of the PDA layer functionalized on the surface of m-SiO2/N-FCDs. The final weight loss of 21 % below 600 °C might be due to the decomposition of N-FCDs and the silica network (Fig. 4f). All these findings suggest the successful encapsulation of N-FCDs into the pores of m-SiO2 and modification of m-SiO2/N-FCDs with the PDA layer.

The XPS spectrum was measured to study the chemical composition of the m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA composites. The four distinct peaks at 530.88, 399.24, 285.93, and 103.64 eV were ascribed to O 1 s, N 1 s, C 1 s, and Si 2p, respectively, in the XPS spectrum (Fig. 5 a). In the C 1 s’s high-resolution fitted peaks spectrum, two peaks at 284.98 and 285.39 correspond to the C–C and C–N binding states, respectively, demonstrating the doping of N into the FCDs. (Fig. 5b). The N 1 s spectrum has peaks at 400.12 and 400.21 eV, corresponding to N–H and C–N–C (Fig. 5c). The spectra of oxygen revealed two peaks at 532.81 and 533.01 eV, allocated to the OC–OH and OC–O groups (Fig. 5d). The Si 2p spectra peak at 102.13 and 104.06 eV, assigned to the Si-O-C/Si-O and SiO2 (Fig. 5e). All the results demonstrate that the modification of m-SiO2/N-FCDs with the PDA layer was successful.

To evaluate the stability of m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA, the effect of pH, time-dependent fluorescence, and storage time on the emission intensity was investigated. The change in fluorescence emission intensity was studied within the pH range of 4 – 10. It was observed that the fluorescence intensity of m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA increases with the increase in pH and reaches a maximum at pH = 7 (Figure S3). It might be due to the reason that under acidic conditions, functional groups (amine, hydroxyl) present on the surface of m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA formed hydrogen bonds, which are prone to aggregation and hence lead to weakened fluorescence emission intensity. A time-dependent fluorescence was studied for 60 min, and there was no noticeable shift in the emission response of the m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA during 60 min (Figure S4). The storage ability of m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA was studied for 7 days, and the findings indicate no appreciable change in the fluorescence emission intensity throughout this period (Figure S5). This indicates that m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA had acceptable storage stability and was suitable for real-world applications.

The fluorescent nature of m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA can be revealed by comparing the emission spectra of m-SiO2, N-FCDs, m-SiO2/N-FCDs, and m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA. As shown in Figure S6, N-FCDs show emission wavelength at 435 nm (λex. = 380 nm). After loading N-FCDs to the m-SiO2, there is a red shift of emission wavelength centered at 520 nm (λex. = 440 nm) with a decrease in emission intensity. This red shift might be due to the interaction between SiOH and the functional groups (hydroxyl and carboxyl) present on the N-FCDs surface, which alters the energy traps and fluorescence properties of N-FCDs. On the other hand, m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA had an overall greater emission intensity than m-SiO2/N-FCDs (Figure S7). The spectrofluorimetric studies of m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA showed quenching of the fluorescence emission (λem = 520 nm) with the addition of NSAIDs (ASP, DCF, KPF, IBF, and NPX) with quenching % of 89.29, 85.02, 84.76, 82.19, and 79.75 %, respectively (Note: The quenching % was calculated using (F₀ − F)/ F₀ × 100, where F₀ denotes initial intensity and final intensity is denoted by F after adding the NSAIDs). The sensitivity performance of the m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA was further investigated by observing its fluorescence intensities by adding different amounts of NSAIDs. As seen in Fig. 6 , the fluorescence emission intensity decreases gradually along with the increasing concentration of ASP, DCF, KPF, NPX, and IBU. The quenching efficiency of m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA by different NSAIDs decreases in the order of ASP > DCF > KPF > NPX > IBU. Two possible interactions could explain this decrease in quenching efficiency: i) π-π interactions between aromatic rings of m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA and NSAIDs and ii) hydrogen bonding between hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amine moieties of NSAIDs and hydroxyl and amine groups of m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA (Fig. 7 ; model analyte − ASP). Considering the structure of NSAIDs (Figure S8), ASP and DCF contain five binding sites for hydrogen bonding and one and two benzene rings for π- π interactions, respectively. However, the oxygen atom of ASP is capable of forming stronger hydrogen bonding than the nitrogen atom of DCF. Hydrogen bonding is believed to be the strong interaction that causes quenching even though DCF has two aromatic rings for π-π interactions. In contrast, KPF and NPX have four bonding sites for hydrogen bonds and two aromatic rings for π-π interactions. The methyl group in NPX causes steric hindrance, which results in less quenching. IBU, which has just three binding sites for hydrogen bonding and one aromatic ring for π-π interactions, showed the least quenching of the fluorescence emission intensity of m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA. Furthermore, the tertiary alkyl moiety in IBU leads to more significant steric hindrance and the lowest quenching of m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA.

The experimental parameters, including the amount of m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA, response time, pH effect, and reproducibility, were optimized before sample analysis. The amount of m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA ranging from 100 – 600 µL was investigated. It was observed that the maximum quenching efficiency of NSAIDs was observed at 500 µL of m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA and thus selected as an optimum amount of m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA (Figure S9). The response time of the m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA sensor for NSAIDs was investigated under different time intervals from 0 to 15 min. The change in fluorescence intensity of NSAIDs increased with time and reached a plateau within 12 min (Figure S10). After 12 min, the change in the quenching efficiency was not significant. The pH effect of the solution on the detection of NSAIDs was studied at pH ranges from 4 to 10. As shown in Figure S11, the quenching efficiency was increased with acidic conditions, and at pH 6, the highest quenching efficiency was observed. This may be attributed by considering the pKa values of ASP, DCF, KPF, NPX, and IBU in the range of 3.5–5.2 [31]. At pH ≤ 6, most of the target NSAIDs are present in the molecular form, and the interaction between m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA and NSAIDs was mainly due to the hydrogen bonding and π- π interactions. Likewise, results were observed from the zeta potential studies of m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA investigated at pH 6, and the zeta potential for m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA was − 34.3 ± 4.2 mV, indicating that its dispersion in water is stable. This confirms that favorable interaction brings m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA and NSAIDs molecules closer together and enhances the quenching efficiency of m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA. At higher pH, the target NSAIDs and m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA get deprotonated, causing less quenching due to the electrostatic repulsion between NSAIDs and m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA. The reproducibility of m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA was investigated by preparing five batches of m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA under identical conditions, as explained in section 2.2. The m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA was used to determine NSAIDs at 33–55 nM concentrations under optimum conditions. The developed m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA showed good reproducibility with an RSD of less than 5 % (Figure S12).

The possible fluorescence quenching mechanism includes resonance energy transfer and dynamic and static quenching. The dynamic quenching mechanism is mainly caused by collision and governed by the linear Stern-Volmer (S-V) (Equation 1):
(1)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
S V
Q
where F0 and F are fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of a quencher, respectively. [Q] is the quencher (NSAIDs) concentration, and Ksv is the Stern-Volmer constant. Figure S13 shows the linear S-V plot for NSAIDs (ASP, DCF, KPF, NPX, and IBU). Figure S13 shows that the S-V plot is non-linear and shows a positive deviation from linearity. This deviation in the S-V plot implies that in addition to dynamic quenching, another quenching mechanism might be involved, such as resonance energy transfer and ground state complex formation. These possibilities can be further analyzed using extended S-V equation 2:
(2)
F 0
F
=
( 1 +
k g
Q
)
( 1 +
K
S V
Q
)

As compared to linear S-V plots, non-linear S-V plots give better results with R2 values in the range of 0.9827–0.997 (Fig. 8 ). The Ksv values for the ASP, DCF, KPF, NPX, and IBU were calculated as 0.006, 0.017, 0.012, 0.011, and 0.008 nM−1, respectively. The effect of temperature on the Ksv was studied at three different temperatures (298, 308, and 318 K). As shown in Table S1, Ksv is inversely correlated with temperature, i.e., it decreases with the increase in temperature. The value of Ksv decreases from 0.006 to 0.003, 0.017 to 0.011, 0.012 to 0.008, 0.011 to 0.007, and 0.008 to 0.004 for ASP, DCF, KPF, NPX, and IBU, respectively from 298 to 318 K. Furthermore, a fluorescence resonance energy transfer could be excluded for the quenching mechanism as there was no overlap between the absorbance of NSAIDs (ASP, DCF, KPF, NPX, and IBU) and emission of m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA (Figure S14). It is inferred that the photoinduced electron transfer process between m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA and NSAIDs is in a non-radiative decay pathway. To further investigate the quenching mechanism, fluorescence lifetime decay studies were carried out (Figure S15). The fluorescence lifetime of m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA was 0.0274 ns which remained unchanged after the addition of NSAIDs (0.0274 ns). This may indicate that electron transfer and static quenching processes cause the quenching phenomenon. The fluorescence quantum yield of m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA was quantified relative to the standard solution of coumarine 153 in ethanol and was calculated as 0.37. The detection limit was obtained based on the ratio of
3 δ
b
(δ = standard deviation of the blank signal, and c = slope) and was found to be 0.045, 0.046, 1.167, 1.308, and 1.435 nM for ASP, DCF, KPF, NPX, and IBU, respectively.

The m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA was utilized for the real-time detection of NSAIDs (ASP, DCF, KPF, NPX, and IBU) in different complex matrices, including tap and river water and pharmaceutical tablets. Tap and river water were analyzed before spiking and found that no NSAIDs was detected in both the water samples. The quantification of NSAIDs in both tap and river water was attempted by spiking known concentrations of NSAIDs. As shown in Table 1 , the relative recovery of NSAIDs in tap and river water were in the range of 97.34 – 100.41 % (RSD% = 0.68 – 3.03) and 96.97 – 100.98 (RSD% = 0.79 – 2.70), respectively. Furthermore, analysis of NSAIDs was done in pharmaceutical tablets and recoveries were found between 90.96 and 96.59 (RSD% = 0.94 – 2.64) (Table 1). These findings suggested that m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA may easily detect NSAIDs in actual samples.

The interference studies of the developed method were studied in light of potential interference cations (Na+, Mg2+, K+, Al3+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Ag+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Cr6+), anions (Cl, SO4 2−, NO3 −, CO3 2−, CH3COO−) and molecules (citric acid, glucose, starch, lactose monohydrate, fructose, ascorbic acid, tetracycline, and oxytetracycline) might be found in tap water, river water, and pharmaceutical formulations. For this, the fluorescence analysis was performed after the addition of interfereing species i.e., cations, anions, and molecules. The amount of interfering species that can alter the fluorescence emission by ± 5 % was considered the tolerance limit. Cations (Na+, Mg2+, K+, Al3+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Fe3+) and anions (Cl-, SO4 2−, NO3 −, CO3 2−, CH3COO−) did not obstruct the analysis of NSAIDs even at 100 times higher concentration than NSAIDs. For the cations (Hg2+, Ag+, Pb2+, Cr6+) and molecules (citric acid, starch, glucose, fructose, lactose monohydrate, ascorbic acid, tetracycline, and oxytetracycline) interference was not noticable upto 50 (Table S2). Hence, no apparent interference from the studied cations, anions, and molecules was observed for the sensing of NSAIDs.

The performance of the proposed method was compared with the other reported methods (high-performance liquid chromatography, electrochemical, and fluorescence) given in the literature (Table 2 ). As shown in Table 2, the mSiO2/N-FCDs/PDA showed a better detection limit of 0.045 – 1.43 nM for NSAIDs than other reported methods. Among the prior sorbents used in high-performance liquid chromatography to isolate NSAIDs, magnetic ionic liquid hyper crosslinked polymer demonstrated the best detection limit, measuring 0.40–1.43 nM [32]. However, sample preparation was a prerequisite to analyzing NSAIDs using this method. Since our detection approach does not require a sample preparation step, it simplifies and accelerates the process. The fluorescence investigation revealed a detection limit of 1.16 nM for ASP utilizing mesoporous molecularly imprinted fluorescent carbon dots [16]. However, this method is limited to identifying one NSAID, whereas mSiO2/N-FCDs/PDA can analyze up to six NSAIDs. Furthermore, the developed method showed a good recovery in the 90.96–100.41 % with an RSD of < 4 %. These results showed that mSiO2/N-FCDs/PDA could be applied for the sensitive and accurate analysis of NSAIDs in real samples, including pharmaceutical and water samples.

An environmentally friendly strategy based on one-pot hydrothermal and self-polymerization was used to synthesize m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA composites effectively. Significantly, luminescent sensing studies show that m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA may be used as a multifunctional sensing platform to detect NSAIDs (ASP, DCF, KPF, NPX, and IBU) in a variety of real-world samples, including tap and river water and pharmaceutical tablets. m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA has much more active anchoring sites, including amines and catechols, resulting in more significant binding to the target NSAIDs. The optimized protocol showed excellent recovery for the NSAIDs in tap water, river water, and pharmaceutical tablets in the range of 97.34 – 100.41 % (RSD% = 0.68 – 3.03), 96.97 – 100.98 (RSD% = 0.79 – 2.70), and 90.96 – 96.59 (RSD% = 0.94 – 2.64), respectively. The results of our approach showed that the detection limits of ASP, DCF, KPF, NPX, and IBU were, respectively, 0.045, 0.046, 1.167, 1.308, and 1.435 nM. This flexible detection platform is expected to provide insights into how NSAIDs interact with m-SiO2/N-FCDs/PDA composites and their effectivemulti-responsive sensor applications for environmental monitoring. However, simultaneously detecting these drugs in a coexisting environment presents additional challenges. Subsequent investigations should concentrate on creating multi-emission fluorescent probes and incorporating two-dimensional chromatography. These developments will greatly improve the sensitivity and specificity of detection, offering the possibility of more precise simultaneous detection of many NSAIDs.
This research did not receive any funding.

Palvi Chandel: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Sunakshi Sharma: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Irshad Mohiuddin: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Shikha Bhogal: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

SB would also like to thank the University Centre for Research and Development of Chandigarh University, Gharaun, Mohali, India, for instrumental facilities.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
